constraints.statement: Knowledge source of truth lives only in mounted stores under ~/.fabric/stores; project-local .fabric/knowledge is not a runtime source.
Personal store is an implicit private layer, not a project required_stores dependency; personal-scope writes target only the personal store.
Skills propose semantic_scope; server validates it and resolver maps it to visibility_store through routes/defaults.
Every non-personal write target must be in the current project's read-set.
Pending entries are discoverable by review/doctor/hints, but not injected into normal recall or plan-context.
MCP user-facing references use alias:id with structured provenance including store_uuid, alias, local_id, semantic_scope, and visibility_store when known.
Scope priority is a relevance tie-break, not a hard override; equal relevance ranks project before team before future shared org scopes before personal.
Projects bound to multiple shared/team stores require explicit write_routes for project-scoped writes; missing route is a hard write error.
A repo/current working context has exactly one active_project; multi-project operation switches active project rather than exposing multiple active project scopes.
Worktrees share binding by default, but explicit isolation uses workspace_binding_id to separate active_project, write_routes, event ledger, and resolved snapshot state under the same project_id.
MCP discovery uses store-local canonical indexes plus binding-level filtered views, with live-scan fallback and fingerprint validation.
Personal-scope or KP-* entries in shared stores are privacy leaks; sync must block push until remediated.
Cross-store event ledger lives in global state per workspace_binding_id, not in store git and not as project-root runtime state.
Because the product has 0 users, legacy project-local knowledge and dual-root runtime fallbacks should be removed; only explicit one-time migration/import remains.
CLI, server, MCP, hooks, skills, docs/tests/i18n must be covered by a surface alignment matrix before store-only is considered complete.
All surfaces use shared schemas/resolvers plus CLI/MCP generated outputs as the single source of truth; no surface hand-writes store/read-set/path rules.
MCP write/review contracts are scope-first: fab_extract_knowledge and review modify use semantic_scope as the primary input, not layer.
CLI onboarding writes write_routes such as project:<id> -> store; active_write_store/switch-write is removed or reduced to a route-writing compatibility alias.
Hooks use global workspace_binding_id state for snapshots, ledgers, metrics, and stats; they do not append project-root .fabric/events.jsonl or scan .fabric/knowledge.
Fabric skills are updated in the same version as runtime to use semantic_scope, store-only pending/review, and global ledger semantics.
Doctor/sync treat store-only contract violations as hard gates with safe --fix paths where possible; sync blocks privacy and invalid route/read-set pushes.
Every surface alignment matrix row must have unit/integration/snapshot or static forbidden-pattern acceptance coverage.

insights.summary: Fabric needs three separate identities: project_id for codebase/workspace identity, active_project for knowledge project scope, and workspace_binding_id for local runtime binding isolation.
Store-local canonical index plus binding-level view supports single-store multi-project and same-project multi-worktree without duplicating indexes per worktree.
A proposed semantic_scope must resolve to a readable writable store before pending creation, preventing write-success/read-invisible states.
Pending knowledge remains a review queue and must not affect normal rule injection before approval.
The architecture is not complete until CLI, server, MCP, hooks, skills, doctor/sync, indexes, ledgers, migration, and tests all pass the same store-only surface matrix.
write_routes are address-based routing for semantic_scope; active_write_store is only a default mailbox model and should not drive multi-team writes.

recommended_next.reason: Map these architectural decisions to modules, schema changes, and implementation risks.
Turn the locked architecture into phased implementation work.
